g.data-narration: Welcome to Java Variables! In this lecture, we'll explore how to store and manipulate data using variables.
A variable is a named memory location that stores a value. Think of it as a labeled box that holds data for your program.
g.text: A variable is a name given to a memory location that stores a value.
It acts like a labeled container for data in your program.

g.data-narration: Just like a jar labeled 'Sugar' contains sugar, a Java variable named 'age' contains the value 25. Variables give meaning to data.
Java has 8 primitive types - the fundamental building blocks: byte, short, int, long, float, double, char, and boolean.
Integer types store whole numbers: byte (-128 to 127), short (-32K to 32K), int (billions), long (very large).
Float and double store decimal numbers. Use float for smaller decimals (add 'f' suffix) and double for precision (default).
Char stores a single character (like 'A' or '$') using 16-bit Unicode. Boolean stores only true or false values.
Reference types store objects - instances of classes, arrays, or interfaces. They reference memory locations, not actual values.
g.text: Reference types store objects (instances of classes, arrays, or interfaces).
They hold a reference (memory address) to the actual object, not the object itself.

g.data-narration: To declare a variable, specify its type followed by a name: 'int age;' This creates a variable without assigning a value yet.
Initialize variables by assigning a value during declaration: 'int age = 25;' This sets up the variable with a starting value.
Local variables are declared inside methods or blocks. They only exist within that scope and must be initialized before use.
g.text: Declared inside methods or blocks. Only accessible within that scope.
Must be initialized before use. No default value.

g.data-narration: Instance variables are declared inside a class but outside methods. Each object gets its own copy. Have default values.
g.text: Declared inside a class, outside all methods. Each object has its own copy.
Automatically initialized to default values (0, false, null).

g.data-narration: Static variables are shared by all objects of a class. Declared with 'static' keyword, one copy exists per class.
g.text: Declared with 'static' keyword. Shared by ALL objects of the class.
Only one copy exists, regardless of how many objects are created.

g.data-narration: Java automatically promotes smaller types to larger types. An int can be assigned to a double without casting - the value is widened.
g.text: Java automatically converts smaller types to larger types when needed.
No data loss occurs - this is safe automatic conversion.
byte → short → int → long → float → double

g.data-narration: Explicit casting converts larger types to smaller types using (type). This can cause data loss - be careful with precision!
g.text: Manually convert larger types to smaller types using (type) cast operator.
WARNING: This can cause data loss - use carefully!

g.data-narration: Let's review what we learned about Java variables: 8 primitive types, 3 variable kinds, declaration, initialization, and type casting.

g.text: int, long, float, double, char, boolean
